texts.orig: Abstract
texts.text: Abstract

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The organic-inorganic hybrids of poly(amide-6-b-ethylene oxide) (PEBAX ® ) and silica were prepared via in situ polymerization of tetraethoxysilane (TEOS) using the sol-gel process and their gas transport properties were studied. The nanodispersed inorganic network produced in the organic matrix was characterized by solid-state 29 Si NMR, Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR), dynamic light scattering (DLS) and field emission scanning electron microphotography (FE-SEM). Wide angle X-ray diffraction (WAXD) patterns and differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) thermograms of PEBAX ® and hybrid materials revealed that the incorporation of silica induced the structural modification of polymer chains. Gas permeation measurements were accomplished at various temperatures with He, CO2, O2 and N2. These hybrid membranes exhibited higher gas permeability coefficients and permselectivities than PEBAX ® , particularly at an elevated temperature. Activation energy of CO2 permeation through the hybrid membranes decreased due to the large contribution of heat of sorption while that of N2 increased resulting from the increase of tortuosity and restricted chain mobility. All these results were explained by the increase of sorption ability of hybrids, attributed to the strong interaction between CO2 molecules and SiO2 domains, additional sorption sites in polyamide block in PEBAX ® and the organic/inorganic interphase. © 2001 Elsevier Science B.V. All rights reserved.

texts.orig: degree of homogeneity can be achieved by inducing the connectivity between the inorganic network and the organic phase, thereby retarding the phase separation. One method involves the use of compatiblizer, which diffuses to the interface between the two immiscible phases and reduces the interfacial tension, thereby improving fine dispersion. Additionally, the compatibilizer can increase the adhesion between the two phases [13,17]. Secondly, the introduction of chemical bonds between the organic and inorganic phase can be induced by utilizing organic polymers functionalized with trialkoxysilane groups or alkoxysilane monomers containing functional groups. This chemical connectivity improves homogeneity and mechanical properties of the resulting composite materials [18-21]. Finally, in situ polymerization of metal alkoxide in organic polymer matrices can be used to nano- or microcomposite materials. In order to achieve a high level of dispersed and homogeneous system, one should choose polymers with an appropriate backbone structure (e.g. carbonyl, hydroxyls or ether oxygens) that can induce physical interactions with the growing inorganic oxide network [22-25].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Recently, a number of groups have reported organic-inorganic hybrids as one of candidates to improve the permeation characteristics [9-16]. At least, two effects of inorganic particles in polymer matrix on permeation properties can be described. First, the strong interaction between polymer and inorganic particle reduces segmental and subsegmental mobility and inhibits segmental packing of polymer. A change in polymer structures contributes to increase diffusivity (by disrupting interchain packing) and diffusivity selectivity (by increasing polymer backbone stiffness), which plays more important role than solubility in determining the productivity of given polymers [9,10,16]. The fact that a polymer with a high glass transition temperature and a large interchain spacing shows high permeability/selectivity properties is consistent with the conclusion of Robeson [26] and many other structure/property studies [27-29]. Second, the interaction between the residual OH groups on the inorganic component and polar gases (such as CO2 and SO2) and the morphological changes can give rise to the increase of solubility associated with an increase of the Henry's contribution [11,12,15].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In the present study, we explore gas transport properties of PEBAX ® -silica hybrids. These composites have been prepared via in situ polymerization of tetraethoxysilane (TEOS) using the sol-gel process. The permeability and permselectivity at various temperatures were obtained for He, CO2, O2 and N2. It is our intention to investigate and discuss the effect of silica incorporation into PEBAX ® on the structural changes and corresponding permeation characteristics.

texts.orig: PEBAX ® free-standing film was prepared by the solvent casting method. PEBAX ® solutions were prepared using alcohol mixture (propanol: n -butanol = 3:1 in w/w) as a solvent with reflux at 80 ◦ C and vigorous stirring. Then 3 wt.% PEBAX ® solution was poured into a Petri dish and dried at 40 ◦ C for 24 h. Finally, PEBAX ® film was dried at 50 ◦ C for another 24 h in a vacuum oven to remove any residual solvent.

texts.orig: For the preparation of PEBAX ® -silica hybrids, the measured amount of TEOS (previously diluted in propanol) was carefully added to a 3 wt.% PEBAX ® solution. A 0.15 M HCl solution, also diluted with propanol, dropped into the PEBAX ® -TEOS solution mixture at 80 ◦ C under reflux with fast agitation, keeping 4:1 water to TEOS hydrolysis ratio. After 30 h reaction, the homogeneous solution was poured into a covered Teflon-coated Petri dish. After 2 weeks at room temperature, the cover was removed and the solvent was evaporated for at least another 48 h. To remove any residual solvent, the film was further dried under vacuum for 24 h.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: 29 Si NMR provides a unique way of confirming the hydrolysis and condensation reactions in the sol-gel process. As the polymerization was progressed, distinct resonances were observed for the silicon units with Q 0 through Q 4 species (increasing the extent of condensation), where the superscript refers to the number of -O-Si groups bonded to the silicon atom of interest [30]. 29 Si solid-state spectrum of selected sample was recorded on a Varian Unity Inova spectrometer at 300 MHz using the magic angle spinning (MAS) technique. The sample was located in a zirconia rotor operating at the spinning rate of 3.5 kHz. The spectral width was 50 kHz and 64 K data points were used. Acquisition time of 0.064 s and a relaxation delay of 10 s were used. The chemical shifts were given with reference to tetramethylsilane (TMS).

texts.orig: package composed of a goniometer, a correlator with the digital 8 bits × 512 channels (maximum), a photomultiplier for photon counting, He-Ne laser operated at 632.8 nm wavelength and 10 mW intensity, thermostatic bath (temperature stability ± 0.05 ◦ C) and an associated software package for data analysis of DLS including Marquadt, NNSL and CUMULANT. The scattering intensity was measured at 90 ◦ . For DLS measurements, dilute solutions were prepared by the direct addition of deionized water, and the cells flame-sealed under mild vacuum. The resulting solutions were stable over the time, showing the same correlation function after several weeks form the initial measurements. The relaxation times obtained using Marquadt fell into two categories: a fast mode caused by the molecular diffusion and a slow mode caused by the internal mode. The relative contribution of the molecular diffusion and the various internal modes depend on qR g, where q is the scattering vector and R g the radius of gyration of the polymer. The detailed description on the evaluation of the apparent hydrodynamic radii, R h, has been reported [31].

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.4. Gas permeation apparatus
texts.text: 2.4. Gas permeation apparatus

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The gas permeation measurements were accomplished using the continuous-flow technique [32-34]. One could measure directly the dynamic permeation properties from the permeation transients in the unsteady state. While the downstream side is continuously pumped, the permeation transients are measured immediately. Measurements based on this technique, however, have several drawbacks, as reported by Yeom et al. [35]. In this study, we used an apparatus that is able to directly measure both flux transient and the composition of the permeate gas and to solve the drawbacks in the continuous-flow technique. The detailed description was described in the previously reported work [35]. The apparatus consisted of a membrane cell with an effective permeation area of 13.8 cm 2 , a gas flow system, a constant temperature bath and a flow measurement equipment. The downstream line is also covered with heating band which is controlled by PID controller. Permeation of gases were measured by a mass flow meter (MFM, Brooks, Tokyo, Japan) with capacities of 100 sccm and the data was recorded on a personal computer. Before the gas permeation measurements, the pressure difference between the upper and the lower side of gas flow meter was maintained at 0.3 atm in order to minimize the pressure drop developed during the permeation experiments. The temperature of permeation cell was changed from 25 to 85 ◦ C during measurement.

texts.orig: The hydrogen bonding interaction between the silicate domains and PEBAX ® was further examined and the results were shown in Fig. 7. Fig. 7(a) shows the spectra of amide group of PEBAX ® in comparison with that of PEBAX ® -silica hybrids. The C=O stretching in amide group of PEBAX ® consists of two types, the free amide C=O peak at 1656 cm -1 and the hydrogen-bonded amide peak at 1640 cm -1 . This indicates that the PA block of PEBAX ® is significantly self-associated via hydrogen bonding. This strong interaction between different PEBAX ® chains induces the microcrystalline structure, as will be discussed later. One observes that a considerable amount of free amide groups contributing to the spectrum of PEBAX ® disappears after an incorporation of silicate. Acareful examination also reveals that the amide peak for hybrid materials shifts to higher frequency, resulting in one strong absorption peak. This suggests that the hydrogen bonding between the amide groups is partially broken by the presence of the silica domains.

texts.orig: In addition, a significant change in N-H stretching region is clearly visible in Fig. 7(b). A strong peak in PEBAX ® at 3290 cm -1 is the hydrogen-bonded N-H peak and the weak 3328 cm -1 peak is a non-hydrogen-bonded N-H vibration. When the silicate is present in PEBAX ® , the free N-H stretching is greatly reduced at the same time the hydrogen bonded N-H stretching shifts to higher frequency. These results, together with the substantial hydration in 3495 cm -1 and a broad band at 1080-1220 cm -1 , indicate that new interactions between the components of the hybrid materials appear after disrupting the existing interchain hydrogen bonding.

WAXD patterns of PEBAX ® -silica hybrids was compared with those of pure silicate and PEBAX ® (Fig. 8). In general, when a polymer contains a large crystalline region, the peak from X-ray diffraction is sharp and the intensity is strong, whereas that of amorphous polymer is rather broad. texts.orig: Table 5 lists the selectivity of various gases. α CO2 / He and α CO2 / N2 increase with the silica content. The reason might be due to the increased solubility selectivity. As the content of silicate in hybrid membrane increases, the interphase and the silica domains available to sorb polar gases increase, as discussed previously. Here, α CO2 / He and α CO2 / N2 increase with the increase of silica content, resulting mainly from the increase of solubility selectivity, while α O2 / N2 does not change much because solubility selectivity of O2/N2 is almost the same.

texts.orig: where P 0 is the pre-exponential factor, E p the ap- parent activation energy for permeation, R the gas constant, and T the absolute temperature. The similar equations can express the temperature dependence of the diffusivity and the solubility of gases in polymer [43]. However, gas permeation of PEO containing polymeric films is strongly dependent on the operating temperature and shows break-points at temperature below and above the melting point of PEO [44]. These are attributed to the increase in the segmental motion of the PEO containing polymers, which most likely results from the influence of the melting of PEO (about 50 ◦ C). The temperature dependence of the permeabilities of penetrant gases through PEBAX ® 1657 film shows a similar behavior to other PEO containing polymers [39]. However, the hybrid material exhibited the different temperature dependence due to the modified PE block in PEBAX ® by the presence of SiO2. As shown in Fig. 12(a), the break-point of PEBAX ® disappears in the hybrid membrane, which is attributed to the decrease of the crystallinity of PE block after the incorporation of SiO2.

texts.orig: Fig. 12(b) shows the changes of α CO2 / N2 with various temperatures at 3 atm. Many researchers have reported that PEO containing films exhibited high P CO2 and α CO2 / N2 for acidic gases because PEO segment can dissolve a large amount of acidic gases [45]. At high temperature, however, the performance of PEO based films dropped steeply because of a significant decrease in the solubility selectivity. For example, α CO2 / N2 decreased as much as 75% for PEO pore-filled polyacrylonitrile membranes with a graft yield of 42.2 [46], 72% for PEBAX ® 1657 films [39], 80% for poly(ether imide) segmented copolymers with PEO content of 68.6 [47] and 90% for PEO/PU blend film with a PEO content of 30 wt.% [44] when temperature was increased from 25 to 80 ◦ C. Although the temperature dependence of α CO2 / N2 in hybrids shows the similar behavior, the degree of decrease was much lower for the hybrids due to the amount of sorption in hybrids was larger than that of PEBAX ® at high temperature, resulting from the strong interaction between CO2 molecules and SiO2 domains and additional sorption sites in PA block. For example, high CO2/N2 permselectivity was shown by P8S2: CO2 permeabiltiy, P CO2 = 507 Barrer and the selectivity of CO2 over N2, α CO2 / N2 = 44 at 85 ◦ C and 3 atm. Because they exhibit high permeability and selectivity even at an elevated temperature, the PEBAX ® -silica hybrids provide one of candidates for industrial applications.

texts.orig: 4. Conclusions
texts.text: 4. Conclusions

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The organic-inorganic hybrids, based on poly(amide-6-b-ethylene oxide) (PEBAX ® ) and silica, were successively prepared via in situ polymerization of tetraethoxysilane (TEOS) using the sol-gel process. The hybrid membranes exhibited higher gas permeability coefficients and permselectivities than those of PEBAX ® , particularly at an elevated temperature: α CO2 / N2 = 72 at 25 ◦ C with P CO2 = 205 Barrer and α CO2 / N2 = 44 at 85 ◦ C with P CO2 = 507 Barrer for P8S2 obtained. The high permeability and permselectivity of the hybrid membranes are attributed to the strong interaction between CO2 molecules and SiO2 domains, additional sorption sites in PA block of PEBAX ® and the organic/inorganic interphase.
texts.text: The organic-inorganic hybrids, based on poly(amide-6-b-ethylene oxide) (PEBAX ® ) and silica, were successively prepared via in situ polymerization of tetraethoxysilane (TEOS) using the sol-gel process. The hybrid membranes exhibited higher gas permeability coefficients and permselectivities than those of PEBAX ® , particularly at an elevated temperature: α CO2 / N2 = 72 at 25 ◦ C with P CO2 = 205 Barrer and α CO2 / N2 = 44 at 85 ◦ C with P CO2 = 507 Barrer for P8S2 obtained. The high permeability and permselectivity of the hybrid membranes are attributed to the strong interaction between CO2 molecules and SiO2 domains, additional sorption sites in PA block of PEBAX ® and the organic/inorganic interphase.
